I've looked at it, and found it pretty well unusable without using something
like a COBOL based
thin client screen handler. (Check out Flexus.) That adds considerable cost
to the project, as
well as complexity.

Support for Microfocus or AcoCOBOL screen sections would not be all that
difficult to
achieve.


-Paul

"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40e33a9c.460864375@news.optonline.net...
> paul@raulersons.com (Paul Raulerson) wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I just learned that Fujitsu sells a Cobol compiler for Linux (on Intel)
for
> $1,500. THERE ARE NO RUNTIME FEES. Features include an integrated SQL
> precompiler,  support for some IBM and Micro Focus extensions, an Eclipse
(open
> source) IDE, support for generating CGI,  and OO.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Applications that have extensive screen interactions may take more effort
to
> port. Depending on the design and purpose of the applications, they may be
able
> to transition to becoming Web-based applications using CGI, or take on new
> graphical user interfaces using a more GUI focused language while
preserving the
> main business logic in COBOL."
>
> They offer free 30-day trial. I'm considering doing two projects with it,
using
> CGI (Web) as a user interface.
>
>
```

Bob's site is actually the one 'out of date'.  He still lists DOS and
OS/2, and also UNIX Motif which he hasn't offered in some time.

"""COBOL sp2 has been continually enhanced to provide new features.
sp2 (and it spredecessor, screenplay) versions and release dates have
been as follows:

    * screenplay v.1.0 - May 1986
    * COBOL sp2 v.1.0 - October 1987
    [..]
    * COBOL sp2 v.3.2 - August 1995 """

Uhhhh, Bob, that's nearly 9 years ago, and its up to release 4.1.xx.
```

openings out there for

with salaries that
range at or above six figures, and with enough interest to induce both a
cross country
move and a career change?

projects - companies
doing very large scale conversions or rapidly acquiring other companies and
so forth. Vacation
and other benefits, as well as stock options, retirement plans, etc.

This posting, just thinking about the small amount of information you
provided, seems
rather uninteresting and targeted to entry level folks. Also, the location
does not exactly
seem attractive - to say the least.

Why not dig out some really interesting jobs to post here.

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-07-05T16:18:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40e97dac.176395383@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <34KEc.18179$x9.2330@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <10eir4dg319ej48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>PAUL RAULERSON wrote:
>> Also, the location does not exactly
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>there would probably be like $100K in Atlanta...

Cost of Living is mostly a function of home ownership. In the Northeast, an
average home costs $400K with property taxes of $8K. In low COL places, both
cost and taxes are 25% as much. But apartments cost about the same $600-800 per
month everywhere (except NYC).

I think contractors are better off in high COL places for three reasons:
.. Wages are higher .. to support those expensive homes (they're not living in).
.. Per diem is much higher -- over $200/day in the NE vs. $80/day in low COL
places. This translates to $15K savings on Federal taxes.
.. More jobs .. the reason _why_ homes are so expensive.

This self-reinforcing dynamic may explain why medium-sized cities (that aren't
suburbs of a big city) are stagnant or in decline while big cities keep getting
bigger. The threshold seems to be 200K population. Many chain stores and
franchisers use 200K as the cutoff. For some reason, college towns and State
Capitols about that size, such as Jackson and Montgomery, do less well than
cities with a non-governmental base, such as Birmingham.

Conversely, retired people are foolish to live near big cities. They're paying a
premium for proximity to high-paying jobs that don't benefit them.
```

(I'm not disagreeing with anything you said above - just throwing out

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=a0b8rq%247f3%241%40panix3.panix.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

(side note - this same approach, when applied to the question of what 
salary it would take to get me to shift from consultant to employee in 
Manhattan, would generate slack-jawed amazement; I would base my figures 
on two facts and one assumption:

Fact: the Federal Housing Authority (FHA) recommends that housing ans 
associated expenses consume no more than 25% - 33% of one's income.

Fact: an average one-bedroom apartment in Manhattan costs (US$x).

Assumption: It is a Good and Proper Thing for a company to pay a skilled, 
technical employee enough to afford at *least* an average, one-bedroom in 
the town in which s/he is expected to work.)

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-07-07T01:15:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40eb4d7c.295149056@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40e97dac.176395383@news.optonline.net> <10ejeqibpv7eq89@corp.supernews.com> <40ea8a4c.245172938@news.optonline.net> <cce52m$9ho$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>(side note - this same approach, when applied to the question of what 
>salary it would take to get me to shift from consultant to employee in 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>--end quoted text

An average one-bedroom apartment in Manhattan costs $2,500/mo. Using 25%, your
salary should be $120K/yr = $60/hr. Using 33%, it should be $45/hr. 

The going rate for Cobol only (plus SQL and common tools) is $45-50/hr. If you
have financial services (banking, brokerage) experience, they _might_ go to $60.
But if you play the per diem card intelligently, the Feds will give you (net)
$20K/yr in tax reduction = $10/hr. Thus, your simple Cobol skillset is worth
$55-60/hr. You can afford to live in Manhattan. 

Many financial services companies have moved their 'back offices' to the
beautiful NJ suburbs, where you can rent a nice apartment for $900/mo (as I do
in Morris County). They pay Manhattan-scale wages. You can make $50/hr =
$100K/yr (before per diem) while paying only 11% for housing.

If you play the per diem card, the IRS says you must move to another city within
365 days. That's what I do. Moving is a strain and seemingly wasteful. I discard
nearly everything and buy all new stuff -- bed, linens, kitchen utensils, dishes
& glasses, furniture, microwave, etc. The cost is only  $1-2K at WalMart prices.
I ship the stuff I want to keep -- kitchen and clothing, no furniture -- via UPS
for about $500.

I've done six gigs in this nomadic mode .. at Chicago, Jacksonville, Montgomery,
Albany NY, Northport NY and Parsippany NJ. Between gigs I sometimes repair to my
'tax home', an apartment in Lubbock TX. 

For this to work, you gotta be mobile .. willing to junk everything and move on
a day's notice. 

Contracting companies always say you must be there at 8am sharp next Monday.
When asked why, they say the client insists. If you can't make it, they'll find
someone else. That's pure BS. The contracting company is afraid you'll get a
better offer in the interim. Twice in my experience, the client said 'We can't
be ready Monday. Tell him to arrive Wednesday.' The contracting company had a
hard time explaining the client's (alleged) change of face.
```

In article <40eb4d7c.295149056@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>$55-60/hr. You can afford to live in Manhattan. 

I can afford to do so *as a consultant*, Mr Wagner... but i do not know 
too many organisations that are taking on employee COBOL-jockies at 
salaries of US$90 - US$120K/yr.  Notice the quote above begins with 'this 
same approach, when applied to the question of *what salary it would take 
to get me to shift from consultant to employee* in Manhattan' (emphasis 
added).

Even more interesting is the shift from renting to owning.  According to 
http://nyc-standard-deviance.blogspot.com/atom.xml :

--begin quoted text:

Today there was a nice little article in the New York Times about the 
average prices of one-bedroom apartments. A one-bedroom apartment in 
Manhattan this year cost an average of $470,000.

--end quoted text

... and using the old rule-of-thumb that motrgage = 3x annual gross and a 
down-payment of 20% this 'average apartment' requires about US$125K/yr... 
again, not something I have seen offered to too many COBOL-jockies as a 
salary... then again, I haven't been looking at such recently.

>
>Many financial services companies have moved their 'back offices' to the
>beautiful NJ suburbs, where you can rent a nice apartment for $900/mo (as I do
>in Morris County). They pay Manhattan-scale wages. You can make $50/hr =
>$100K/yr (before per diem) while paying only 11% for housing.

I believe that I've sung this song well enough to improvise harmony, Mr 
Wagner; before the Great Jersey Migration had taken place I lived in 
Newark and took the bus into Port Authority.

(This allows me to say now 'I lived in Newark, Nw Jersey for six years... 
now I fear nothing.')

>
>If you play the per diem card, the IRS says you must move to another city within
>365 days.

If you are an employee, Mr Wagner, there is no per diem card to play... 
hence the 'slack-jawed amazement' mentioned above.

[snip]

>Contracting companies always say you must be there at 8am sharp next Monday.
>When asked why, they say the client insists. If you can't make it, they'll find
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>hard time explaining the client's (alleged) change of face.

Well... in my experience, yes and no.  In general... if I am not at a 
desk, pulling down billable hours, within two working days of the manager 
getting a fax of my brag-sheet I consider the slot to be lost; that's the 
schedule I usually see... the pimp calls me (or at leas the better ones do 
to prevent double-submitting), a fax goes to the manager, I get a 
phone-tech or I drop by for a hairy-ears interview, answer a few 
questions, shake a hand, show up and wait a week or two for a log-on ID.

What has happened, at times, is that the pimp has gotten back and said 
'They love you, they need you, they want you... the project starts in (n) 
weeks and I need a committment from you to be there.'

'Oh... who pays me for those (n) weeks?'

'Uhhhhhh... nobody, but it is a really great slot!'

'Yes... what guarantee is there that they won't cancel the project three 
days before they want me to start?'

'Oh, they wouldn't do *that*!'

'I'm sure of it... so you want me to call the other agents, tell them to 
stop looking for slots for me and take myself off the market at my own 
expense for an unguaranteed slot?'

'Well... I wouldn't put it *that* way but...'

'Well... I *would* put it that way and I have no reason to put it any 
other way... but I'm a Reasonable Coder, here's my offer: I'll meet you 
halfway.  I don't care who pays for it... you, the client, Santa Claus, as 
long as the checks clear the bank... but how about I take myself off the 
market, as you request, right after I start getting checks for half of my 
projected billables?  I'll be getting $x/hr for 40hrs/wk... that's 
$40x/wk so let's call it $20x/wk until I start working there, how's that?'

'Until you start there?  But what happens if they cancel the project?'

'Oh, they wouldn't do *that*, would they?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ea8a4c.245172938@news.optonline.net> <cce52m$9ho$1@panix5.panix.com> <40eb4d7c.295149056@news.optonline.net> <ccgp7g$em5$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <40eb4d7c.295149056@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>>have financial services (banking, brokerage) experience, they _might_ go to
$60.
>>But if you play the per diem card intelligently, the Feds will give you (net)
>>$20K/yr in tax reduction = $10/hr. Thus, your simple Cobol skillset is worth
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>added).

Your emphasis is right, and appreciated. A contractor can afford to live in
Manhattan, an employee cannot. Employees must live in Brooklyn, Queens or NJ and
put up with a one-hour commute.

An interesting article in the Wall Street Journal a few months ago cited an
academic report saying average commute TIME has been constant since the days of
the Romans. It is a half hour. Distance has changed due to technology -- cars go
faster than chariots -- but human pain threshold is constant. Here in New
Jersey, many of my cohorts commute 60-100 miles, which takes 1-2 hours. They
come from Connecticut, Pennsylvania and South Jersey. 

>Even more interesting is the shift from renting to owning.  According to 
>http://nyc-standard-deviance.blogspot.com/atom.xml :
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>--end quoted text

That's purchase price. You get it back when you sell (unless the bubble bursts).
The annual expense is property taxes and debt service (interest). Some of my
colleagues pay more in property tax than I pay in rent.

>... and using the old rule-of-thumb that motrgage = 3x annual gross and a 
>down-payment of 20% this 'average apartment' requires about US$125K/yr... 
>again, not something I have seen offered to too many COBOL-jockies as a 
>salary... then again, I haven't been looking at such recently.

Their solution is to have the wife work at a moderately high-paying job.
Combined income tops $125K/yr. 

In the old days, the wife functioned as a backup system. In the unlikely event
the man couldn't work, the wife could take a job as a last resort. Yuppies have
lost that flexibility. If _either_ partner cannot work, they're in failure mode.
That's why bankruptcies are up every year .. to well over 1M. 

>I believe that I've sung this song well enough to improvise harmony, Mr 
>Wagner; before the Great Jersey Migration had taken place I lived in 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>now I fear nothing.')

I'm not easily frightened. I was in USMC Special Ops. I lived in Camden for a
year. I visited Brooklyn last weekend to watch the fireworks. I've traveled
Chicago's South Side extensively. The ONLY time I was frightened enough to lock
my car doors was driving through Newark at midnight in the '70s. It was like
something out of a Capolla movie .. primal.

>Well... in my experience, yes and no.  In general... if I am not at a 
>desk, pulling down billable hours, within two working days of the manager 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>questions, shake a hand, show up and wait a week or two for a log-on ID.

I've heard about the delay in obtaining a logon, and seen it in one place. The
other five out of six had me setup on day one. 

Apparently, you worked at places where quality of management was subnormal.

When I was a full-time employee, before becoming a contractor, every single
place I worked went bankrupt. It wasn't my fault, but I used to think along the
line of Goucho Marx's "I wouldn't want to be a member of a club that would admit
someone like me." I wasn't the problem, but the same defective hiring practices
produced employees who were. 

>What has happened, at times, is that the pimp has gotten back and said 
>'They love you, they need you, they want you... the project starts in (n) 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>'Oh, they wouldn't do *that*, would they?'

I don't sweat such uncertainties. Big companies have inertia that guarantees Big
Projects will move forward. The pimp is as vulnerable as you are, and just as
short on security. 

I once hired a programmer who insisted I put the offer in writing. Why? She
wasn't giving up another position; she was unemployed at the time. It was
legalistic paranoia. People have mistaken ideas about how the system works and
how to protect themselves.
```

>
>>... and using the old rule-of-thumb that motrgage = 3x annual gross and a 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Combined income tops $125K/yr. 

That solution violates the 'one assumption' given at the top of all this, 
Mr Wagner.

>
>In the old days, the wife functioned as a backup system. In the unlikely event
>the man couldn't work, the wife could take a job as a last resort.

In the Oldene Dayse some thought that women were suited only to be wives, 
nurses, librarians or prostitutes... and some of those some sat in the 
hiring-office.

>Yuppies have
>lost that flexibility. If _either_ partner cannot work, they're in failure mode.
>That's why bankruptcies are up every year .. to well over 1M. 

Really?  I thought bankruptcies were up because they were easier to obtain 
legally... no, wait, that can't be, then the responsibility for more 
bankruptcies might be found in the simple laws of supply and demand than 
in moralising about Yuppies.  Let me try again...

Really?  I thought bankruptcies were up because the overall social stigma 
surrounding them decreased; in 'It's a Wonderful Life' George Bailey 
screams as ol', drunk Uncle Billy 'Do you realise what this means?  It 
means baknruptcy and scandal and prison!' and now... well, one of the 
things The Economist mentioned as a Good Thing was how considering 
bankruptcy as more of a 'duelling scar' (as in the US) and less of a 
Scarlet Letter (as in England) allows productive people who have made 
errors another chance... no, wait, that's no good either, there's no 
moralising about Yuppies in that.

>
>>I believe that I've sung this song well enough to improvise harmony, Mr 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>something out of a Capolla movie .. primal.

When I lived there it was a city of 300,000 souls - and more than a few 
probably escaped the census-tallyers - that did not have a first-run 
cinema.  A new supermarket opening was cause enough for the mayor to show 
up to cut the ribbon.

>
>>Well... in my experience, yes and no.  In general... if I am not at a 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Apparently, you worked at places where quality of management was subnormal.

No, Mr Wagner... if most places I worked at were like that then - at least 
according to my experience - management practises which caused a 
consultant to waste a week waiting for a log-on ID were *normal*... at 
least for me they are.

[snip]

>>'Oh, they wouldn't do *that*, would they?'
>
>I don't sweat such uncertainties. Big companies have inertia that guarantees Big
>Projects will move forward. The pimp is as vulnerable as you are, and just as
>short on security. 

Situations like that cause me little perspiring as well, Mr Wagner... but 
since I have seen projects cancelled (or have been told they were 
cancelled) before they started I've developed a simple guideline, 
something like 'The best way to facilitate my performing in an action 
which agrees with your desires is to pay me.'

Want me to code?  No problem, just make sure your checks don't bounce.

Want me to stop looking for another job while you make up your mind about 
something?  No problem, just make sure your checks don't bounce.

>
>I once hired a programmer who insisted I put the offer in writing. Why? She
>wasn't giving up another position; she was unemployed at the time. It was
>legalistic paranoia. People have mistaken ideas about how the system works and
>how to protect themselves. 

I've found the best protection to be on the receiving end of a check which 
clears the bank; this might be a variant of that hoary aphorism - used as 
a title for a collection of short stories by Jean Shepherd - of 'In God we 
trust, all others pay cash.'

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-07-09T08:22:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ccm2mg$kdl$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ec9540.379069523@news.optonline.net> <cci712$dvu$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>real issue -- yuppies are going broke in record numbers. 

I'm not sure what portion of total bankruptcies are incurred by 'yuppies', 
Mr Wagner, and what by other segments of the population... and I am not 
sure how these portions translate into per-capita bankruptcies.  What I 
addressed was the blanket assertion you made of 'That's why bankruptcies 
are up every year.'

>
>>>Apparently, you worked at places where quality of management was subnormal.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>They're not 'normal' for me.

Then perhaps, Mr Wagner, we might be a bit more wary about using the word 
'normal' in the future and stick with the less-ambiguous and 
nonstatistical 'In my experience...'

>How hard is it to anticipate a new worker and
>assign him a userid? It shouldn't take longer than an hour.

What 'should be', Mr Wagner, and what I have seen as 'is' are not 
necessarily the same.  Consider one of the Zen-like statements my Sainted 
Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the angels! - used to torture... 
errrrr, stimulate our young minds with:

'How long does a twenty-minute bus ride take?'

'What do you mean, Grandpa?'

'Just what I said... how long does a twenty-minute bus ride take?'

'But you just said... twenty minutes.'

'And what happens if there's an accident?'

'What do you mean?'

'Just what I said... the bus is driving down the road and two cars ahead 
there's a fender-bender; what happens?'

'Uhhhhh... the traffic stops?'

'It stops... or at least slows down.  Now, what happens when all the 
traffic-lights go against the bus?'

'Uhhhhh... it slows down?'

'Right... and what happens when there's a tent-revival show in town and 
the bus-driver has to allow time for all the cripples and amputees and 
dead people to get on so they can get to their Big Meeting?'

'It slows down some more.'

'*Right*!  And with all this slowing-down... does the ride take more or 
less time?'

'More time, Grandpa... but how much more?'

'Nobody knows... so, how long does a twenty-minute bus ride take?  As long 
as it has to.'

(Another of his favorites was 'What time does the 11:05 train leave the 
station?')

[snip]

>>Want me to stop looking for another job while you make up your mind about 
>>something?  No problem, just make sure your checks don't bounce.
>
>They could still cancel the project on a day's notice. I've not encountered that
>but I know it happens. 

Nothing I know of can eliminate all uncertainty, Mr Wagner... isn't a 
prison the only place for Maximum Security?

>
>A check is no protection from management indecision. 

I do not ask for protection from such... just for something to keep the 
landlord from considering eviction-proceedings.  As the French used to 
say, 'Liebe is schoen, aber Liebe mit Brot besser ist'... likewise, 
management indecision is part of Life, management indecision with a check 
that has cleared the bank makes things a bit easier.

(This may be seen as a variant of a sentiment expressed by another obscure 
philosopher: 'Iron bars do not a prison make... but they sure help, eh, 
doc?' - B Bunny)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:

>>>>lost that flexibility. If _either_ partner cannot work, they're in failure
mode.
>>>>That's why bankruptcies are up every year .. to well over 1M. 
>>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>are up every year.'

Most bankruptcies come from the underclass. That number is fairly constant. The
reason total bankruptcies is on the increase is due to the addition of
middle-class yuppies. They didn't declare bankruptcy in the past; now they do. 

Why? Not because it's easier or more acceptable. In fact, it's more difficult.
The reason is easy credit, poor money management skills and a consumer
mentality. They take a vacation in the Bahamas financed by credit cards, or they
go to a plastic DisneyWorld. These charges accumulate until they can only pay
the $200/month interest at 24%. They are treading water. When either partner
cannot work, they crash and burn. 

>>>>Apparently, you worked at places where quality of management was subnormal.

>>How hard is it to anticipate a new worker and
>>assign him a userid? It shouldn't take longer than an hour.
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>as it has to.'

I often take the Lakeland bus from Parsippany to Manhattan. It is scheduled to
take 45 minutes and arrives like clockwork in somewhat less than 45 minutes.
Apparently Lakeland management needs education in your Grandpa's Zen wisdom.
```

```
In article <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>Most bankruptcies come from the underclass. That number is fairly constant.

Factual assertion here, Mr Wagner; please provide a cite.

>The
>reason total bankruptcies is on the increase is due to the addition of
>middle-class yuppies.

Repetition of assertion, Mr Wagner; please provide a cite.

>They didn't declare bankruptcy in the past; now they do. 

Another assertion.  If by 'in the past' you mean prior to the Bankruptcy 
Reform Act of 1978... well, that was the Act to which I referred above 
when I stated that 'bankruptcies are easier to obtain legally'.

>
>Why? Not because it's easier or more acceptable. In fact, it's more difficult.

Another assertion, Mr Wagner.  What legal impediments exist that make it 
'more difficult'?  If you are referring to the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention 
and Consumer Protection Act of 2003 (HR975)... it passed the House but I 
cannot find evidence that it passed the Senate.  I'd be interested in 
learning more.

>The reason is easy credit, poor money management skills and a consumer
>mentality. They take a vacation in the Bahamas financed by credit cards, or they
>go to a plastic DisneyWorld. These charges accumulate until they can only pay
>the $200/month interest at 24%. They are treading water. When either partner
>cannot work, they crash and burn. 

They make simple generalisations, argue by assertion and repetition and... 
oh, no, wait, most folks in this newsgroup are too old to be Yuppies.

Facts, Mr Wagner, please?

[snip]

>>'Nobody knows... so, how long does a twenty-minute bus ride take?  As long 
>>as it has to.'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Apparently Lakeland management needs education in your Grandpa's Zen wisdom.

Apparently, Mr Wagner, others need education that one swallow doth not a 
summer make and that a bus ride does not necessarily equal a bus ride... 
or else a Greyhound from Miami to Bangor would be identical to the 34th St 
Crosstown.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-07-11T01:43:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f071e5.174636996@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net> <ccm2mg$kdl$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net> <cconlq$g2o$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>lost that flexibility. If _either_ partner cannot work, they're in failure
mode.
>>>>>>That's why bankruptcies are up every year .. to well over 1M. 
>>>>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>Your response was well written and entertaining, but it doesn't deal with
the
>>>>real issue -- yuppies are going broke in record numbers. 
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Factual assertion here, Mr Wagner; please provide a cite.

Personal bankruptcies:
2004 1,618,062 
2003 1,573,720
2002 1,464,961 
2001 1,271,865
2000 1,263,096
1999 1,378,071 
http://www.bankruptcyaction.com/USbankstats.htm

"The profile of the average debtor who files personal bankruptcy has changed.
Wealthy individuals seldom file. The poorest segment of our population seldom
files with the US Bankruptcy Courts. The typical debtor in personal bankruptcy
is an average income earner who either: 1) lost employment, 2) recently
contested divorce, or 3) suffered debilitating injuries or illnesses. Of all
people filing personal bankruptcy, 80% fit into at least one of these
categories."

http://www.personal-bankruptcy-chapters-7-13-filing-laws.com/

"An overwhelmingly large proportion of those filing for bankruptcy (over
two-thirds) have recently experienced a job disruption."

Kartik Athreya, Unemployment Insurance and Personal Bankruptcy
Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond Economic Quarterly Volume 89/2 Spring 2003

I doubt there was an 24% increase in divorce, injury or sickness in the last
five years. [See adjustment factor for couples below.] Therefore, the increase
is coming from workers who lost their jobs. Why would a worker file bankruptcy
just because he or she lost a job? Isn't unemployment insurance supposed to
cover that? It does for people low on the socioeconomic scale. It does not for
people with a house payment in the thousands, two car payments and multiple
credit cards. An unemployment payment of $300 per week will not support a
mid-to-upper middle-class lifestyle. 

This is why I contend the increase is coming from 'yuppies'.

>>The
>>reason total bankruptcies is on the increase is due to the addition of
>>middle-class yuppies.
>
>Repetition of assertion, Mr Wagner; please provide a cite.

Read Elizabeth Warren's "The Two Income Trap: Why Middle-class Mothers & Fathers
are Going Broke".

From a tout: "This important work by a credible scholar explores the precarious
financial architecture of America's middle class, and why it is collapsing."

From the review: "Stay-at-home mothers once provided a financial safety net if
disaster struck; their move into the workforce has left today's families
chillingly at risk. The authors show why the usual remedies--child-support
enforcement, subsidized daycare, and higher salaries for women--won't solve the
problem, and propose a set of innovative solutions, from rate caps on credit
cards to open-access public schools, to restore security to the middle class."

>Another assertion, Mr Wagner.  What legal impediments exist that make it 
>'more difficult'?  If you are referring to the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention 
>and Consumer Protection Act of 2003 (HR975)... it passed the House but I 
>cannot find evidence that it passed the Senate.  I'd be interested in 
>learning more.

I thought it was enacted in 2003. Now I see you're correct; it did not pass the
Senate. 

>>The reason is easy credit, poor money management skills and a consumer
>>mentality. They take a vacation in the Bahamas financed by credit cards, or
they
>>go to a plastic DisneyWorld. These charges accumulate until they can only pay
>>the $200/month interest at 24%. They are treading water. When either partner
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Facts, Mr Wagner, please?

"There were 1,625,208 personal bankruptcies filed in the year ended December 31,
2003. So that means that 1,625,208 people filed bankruptcy. Right? Wrong!
The statistics include joint filings, for example for husband and wife. In
accordance with a study reported in September, 2001, Young, Old, and in Between:
Who Files for Bankruptcy? By Dr. Teresa
Sullivan, Dr. Deborah Thorne and Professor Elizabeth Warren 31.9 % of the
filings for the year ended June 30, 2001 were joint filings of husband and wife.

To approximate the number of people filing bankruptcy we must increase the
filings by 31.9% to get 2,144,000 people who filed bankruptcy in the year ended
December 31, 2003"

http://www.bankruptcyaction.com/USbankstats.htm

[I cannot explain the slight difference in the two numbers for 2004.
www.uscourts.gov/Press_Releases/pr02252004.pdf says 1,625,208.]
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net> <cconlq$g2o$1@panix5.panix.com> <40f071e5.174636996@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40f071e5.174636996@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.personal-bankruptcy-chapters-7-13-filing-laws.com/

Mr Wagner, this is rather odd... 'The typical debtor in personal 
bankruptcy is an average income earner who either...'

'... an average income earner'.  Mr Wagner, a 'Yuppie' is, by definition, 
a 'young, upwardly-mobile professional' or 'someone under 40 who prospered 
during the 1980s'.

So, Mr Wagner, your source says 'average person' while the word you use is 
'Yuppie'... it must be concluded, then, that for your source to support 
your assertion a 'Yuppie' is 'an average person' or 'an average person' 
was born in 1958 or earlier (assuming that those 'who prospered during the 
1980s' were primarily college graduates and college is completed by age 
22... and that 'the 1980s' began 1 Jan 1980).

According to the CIA Factbook 
(http://www.odci.gov/cia/publications/factbook/geos/us.html) the median 
age in the United States is 36; this seems awfully close to the 'average 
age' of 35 bandied about on various web-pages.  An 'average person, 
therefore, cannot - at least by my understanding of arithmetic, have been 
born prior to 1959.  A 'Yuppie, by definition of age, is not 'average'.

According to this same page the percentage of the population in 
'managerial, professional and technical' occupations is 31%.  While this 
is the largest individual grouping it is still less than a third... so 
assuming that it is all 'professional' it does not constitute an average.

As for 'average income'... this is a tricky one to define and I'll back 
off on it; I will assume - for the sake of my argument - that a 'Yuppie' 
individual or household does not, by definition, generate 'average 
income'.

In short, Mr Wagner, your cite does not appear to support your assertions 
that 'bankruptcies are up every year due to Yuppies' when it states that 
the typical filer is 'an average wage earner'.

>
>"An overwhelmingly large proportion of those filing for bankruptcy (over
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond Economic Quarterly Volume 89/2 Spring 2003

Lose your job and file for bankruptcy?  Say it ain't so, Joe!

>
>I doubt there was an 24% increase in divorce, injury or sickness in the last
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>mid-to-upper middle-class lifestyle. 

A mid-to-upper middle-class lifestyle is not 'average', Mr Wagner, and the 
source you cited states the typical filer is 'an average wage earner'.

>
>This is why I contend the increase is coming from 'yuppies'.

It'd be nice to see a source that supports this contention, aye.

[snip of 'a book supports me']

>>Another assertion, Mr Wagner.  What legal impediments exist that make it 
>>'more difficult'?  If you are referring to the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Senate. 

Good of you to admit this, Mr Wagner... now all that needs to be done is 
to make these 'Yuppies' into average folks and your assertions might be 
seen to have documentary support.

DD
```

>>"The profile of the average debtor who files personal bankruptcy has changed.
>>Wealthy individuals seldom file. The poorest segment of our population seldom
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>during the 1980s'.

No, Yuppie is a generational sobriquet for people born between 1944 and 1964.
They didn't like 'hippie', so they put a positive spin by calling themselves
Yuppies. They are now 40-60 years old .. no longer young. They now call
themselves Baby Boomers, or simply Boomers.

>So, Mr Wagner, your source says 'average person' while the word you use is 
>'Yuppie'... it must be concluded, then, that for your source to support 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>22... and that 'the 1980s' began 1 Jan 1980).

See above.

>According to the CIA Factbook 
>(http://www.odci.gov/cia/publications/factbook/geos/us.html) the median 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>born prior to 1959.  A 'Yuppie, by definition of age, is not 'average'.

A (former) Yuppie is older and above average income.

>According to this same page the percentage of the population in 
>'managerial, professional and technical' occupations is 31%.  While this 
>is the largest individual grouping it is still less than a third... so 
>assuming that it is all 'professional' it does not constitute an average.

Forget 'professional' in the definition of Yuppie. It's meaningless. Everyone
who works for a salary is a 'professional'.

>As for 'average income'... this is a tricky one to define and I'll back 
>off on it; I will assume - for the sake of my argument - that a 'Yuppie' 
>individual or household does not, by definition, generate 'average 
>income'.

I argue that bankruptcy filers are above average. An 'average income' person can
subsist on unemployment; an above-average person cannot.

>In short, Mr Wagner, your cite does not appear to support your assertions 
>that 'bankruptcies are up every year due to Yuppies' when it states that 
>the typical filer is 'an average wage earner'.

I dispute the source's assertion. I believe there is a bi-modal distribution
between lower-middle-class and upper-middle-class.

>Lose your job and file for bankruptcy?  Say it ain't so, Joe!

My first impression was to say bankruptcy is off-topic here. Having just checked
DICE and Net-Temps for non-mainframe Cobol, I'm not so sure. In a time when jobs
are florishing, demand for Cobol programmers is poor. The few jobs offered are
either Support positions, low-paid (<$30/hr) or ask for specialized skills.
Nobody seems to be doing new Cobol development.

>>This is why I contend the increase is coming from 'yuppies'.
>
>It'd be nice to see a source that supports this contention, aye.

The data appears to be in books unavailable online.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-07-13T09:07:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cd11bl$1lno$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net> <cconlq$g2o$1@panix5.panix.com> <40f071e5.174636996@news.optonline.net> <ccretd$iqh$1@panix5.panix.com> <40f31ff8.98782601@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert.deletethis@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:40f31ff8.98782601@news.optonline.net...

> No, Yuppie is a generational sobriquet for people born between 1944 and
1964.
> They didn't like 'hippie', so they put a positive spin by calling
themselves
> Yuppies.

I found this in the Merriam-Webster's online dictionary:

      Main Entry: yup�pie
      Pronunciation: 'y&-pE
      Function: noun
      Usage: often capitalized
      Etymology: probably from young urban professional + -ie
      : a young college-educated adult who is employed in a well-paying
profession and who lives and works in or near a large city


And this in the Compact Oxford English Dictionary (also online):

yuppie (also yuppy).  noun (pl. yuppies) informal, derogatory:  a well-paid
young middle-class professional working in a city.  DERIVATIVES yuppiedom
noun; yuppify verb.  ORIGIN from the acronym young urban professional.

Both definitions seem to indicate that there are three qualifying
requirements for a Yuppie:  (1) a yuppie is *young*; (2) a Yuppie works in a
city of significant size; (3) the occupation of a Yuppie can legitimately be
termed a *profession*.

> A (former) Yuppie is older and above average income.

If someone is no longer young he is no longer a yuppie, according to the
dictionary definition.  At the age of 60 I can call myself a Yuppie, just as
I can call myself a rutabaga if such be my pleasure.  Calling myself either
one does not necessarily make it so.

> Forget 'professional' in the definition of Yuppie. It's meaningless.
Everyone
> who works for a salary is a 'professional'.

No, that's not what the dictionaries tell me.  Webster's Ninth Collegiate
has this for "profession":  "a calling requring specialized knowledge and
often long and intensive academic preparation", and for "professional" in
this context:  "engaged in one of the learned professions" and "having a
particular profession as a permanent career".

Being paid a salary is not sufficient to define one as a professional in
one's line of work.

All of this is unless, of course, you are asserting that your command of
common English usage and understanding of common English semantics is
superior to that of the editors at Merriam-Webster and at Oxford University
Press, and consequently we should give the opinions of said editors all the
contempt they so richly deserve.  If that's the case, rest assured, I shall
not fail to do so.

    -Chuck Stevens

`
end
```

In article <40f31ec8.98478627@news.optonline.net>,
 robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Some were created by scholars, who think in abstract terms such as 'utility'.


"http://www.personal-bankruptcy-chapters-7-13-filing-laws.com/" was 
created by Personal Bankruptcy Tips, Inc. with no mention of scholars or 
creditors.

"http://www.bankruptcyaction.com/" is a clearing house for lawyers to 
list themselves for hire to clients.  Also no mention of scholars.

Those were your only two site cites.


> >If it was balance, instead of validation, that you were looking for, you 
> >might try some national bankers associations to see what their thoughts 
> >on the problem are.
> 
> Balance? Gimme a break.

It would be unusual to look at both sides of things.


> >My personal opinion -- people are no longer ashamed of bankruptcy mixed 
> >with it being too easy to walk away from debt.
> 
> People are tired of paying 18% when the Fed Funds Discount Rate is 1%. Can you
> say greedy?

Then why do those people engage in behavior that would get them an 18% 
rate?  Late payments,  history of charged off debt, unsatisfied 
judgements, etc.  That 18% is there to cover the very real risk that 
they will default on a loan.  Anyone without those things can get 9-12% 
on a signature loan or credit card in this market.  Less if they have a 
long relationship with the bank.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-07-12T08:48:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-293D46.08485412072004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ec9540.379069523@news.optonline.net> <cci712$dvu$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net> <ccm2mg$kdl$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net>,
 robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Most bankruptcies come from the underclass. That number is fairly constant. 
> The
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> cannot work, they crash and burn. 

I kinda have to chime in here.  Off and on for 10 years, I've been 
writing, install and  maintaining software to predict and/or mitigate 
risk of bankruptcy.  I've studied it a bit.

The reason they are up is that bankruptcy laws are not what they once 
were.  Exemptions were put in place to mitigate some of the really 
terrible things that would happen in legitimate bankruptcy cases.

Mostly, you get to keep your primary residance, your auto and the first 
$10,000 in personal property (varies a bit by state) before any creditor 
can even think about any recovery.  Unsecured credit card debt is the 
easiest thing in the world to get rid of.

There is a whole subindustry of lawyering that has sprung up to handle  
no-asset bankruptcy for people who have run up their cards and want to 
wipe them out and do it again.  They have learned how to "game" the 
system of exemptions that were put in place for legitimate bankruptcy 
issues.

You've seen their adds, usually right after they say "Had a wreck, I'll 
get you a check!" they say something like "And I can stop those bill 
collectors from bothering you too!".  "Just call Ken Nugent at 
1-800-FRE-CASH".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-07-13T00:17:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f31ec1.98471169@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ec9540.379069523@news.optonline.net> <cci712$dvu$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net> <ccm2mg$kdl$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-293D46.08485412072004@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net>,
> robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> difficult.

>I kinda have to chime in here.  Off and on for 10 years, I've been 
>writing, install and  maintaining software to predict and/or mitigate 
>risk of bankruptcy.  I've studied it a bit.

Fair Isaac FICO?

>The reason they are up is that bankruptcy laws are not what they once 
>were.  Exemptions were put in place to mitigate some of the really 
>terrible things that would happen in legitimate bankruptcy cases.

Are you referring to Deprizio cases? The Bankruptcy Reform Act of 1994 reversed
that decision. 

Creditors present one side of the story, while 'forgetting' court decisions that
went in their favor. For instance, the Act says a court can recover payments
made to insiders up to one year before filing. Courts/States have extended the
limit to between two and four years. 

For example, suppose someone has $30K in unsecured credit card debt and $60K in
cash. He and spouse 'give' the $60K to three trusted relatives (three to avoid
Federal gift tax), then wait a year and a day before filing bankruptcy. Of
course, the relatives will 'give' the money back after the bankruptcy is final.
Under the Act, that was legal. After the Act was modified by courts, a court
could recover the money and pay it to the card companies.

A simpler tactic would have been to been to pay down a home mortgage by $60K
more than 90 days before filing. Then take out a pre-arranged Second after it is
final.

>There is a whole subindustry of lawyering that has sprung up to handle  
>no-asset bankruptcy for people who have run up their cards and want to 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>1-800-FRE-CASH".

At least half those ads are for 'debt consolidation' scams that don't involve
bankruptcy. The con says he will negotiate a lower interest rate in exchange for
supervised repayments via him. He negotiates it down to 6% but tells the mark
the best he could get was 12%. He keeps the other 6% as his 'commission' ..
outside the knowledge of creditor or debtor. What the mark (debtor) doesn't
realize is that he could have gotten 6% on his own, just as easily.

Car dealers play the same game .. with the lender's acquiesence. They negotiate
a 6% loan with GMAC, Ford Credit, etc. while telling the mark the best they
could get was 12%. The mark gets a coupon book reading 12% and pays it directly
to the lender. He doesn't realize that, under the table, the lender has paid the
dealer the present value of the inflated cash flow. About 80% of cars financed
in the US are done this way. Average kickback is about $1K. For high-ticket
cars, it can go as high as $6K. The friendly helpful 'loan consultant' in the
car dealer's back room usually isn't paid a salary. He's on straight commission
for a percentage of the kickback.

That's why consumer advocates tell you to negotiate a loan with your bank BEFORE
visiting the car dealer. It's good advice.

I hear Doc shouting 'Substantiation, Mr Wagner'. For obvious reason, it's not
publically available. Personally, I pay cash, which confuses car salespeople.
They're so attuned to getting a credit application, they don't understand the
meaning of 'cash'. When I push the application aside saying 'no steenking
badges', they have to check with their manager before saying ok.<chuckle>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-07-12T20:42:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C812F5.20425212072004@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <40ec9540.379069523@news.optonline.net> <cci712$dvu$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ee04ae.15581428@news.optonline.net> <ccm2mg$kdl$1@panix5.panix.com> <40ef3db6.95729421@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-293D46.08485412072004@corp.supernews.com> <40f31ec1.98471169@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <40f31ec1.98471169@news.optonline.net>,
 robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> >There is a whole subindustry of lawyering that has sprung up to handle  
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> realize is that he could have gotten 6% on his own, just as easily.

I was thinking more of the lawyers that will file a bankruptcy to get a 
stay and notification of all the creditors.  Then ask that the 
bankruptcy be dismissed.

No harm, no foul and no credit record.  A person and their lawyer can do 
this once every six months for about a $200 court cost. 

All the creditors get a "stay" against any kind of collection activity 
until the next creditor hearing -- anywhere from one to four months from 
notification.  Then it takes one to two months to have the paperwork 
complete for the dismissal.  

In another month or two, with another $200 (plus whatever the lawyer 
charges), the deadbeat can do it again.  This leave the creditors in 
limbo -- the debt is still owed, but efforts to collect are a violation 
of law on the creditors part.
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> An interesting article in the Wall Street Journal a few months ago cited an
> academic report saying average commute TIME has been constant since the days of
> the Romans. 

I wasn't aware that the Romans commuted to Wall Street.

> It is a half hour. Distance has changed due to technology -- cars go
> faster than chariots 

Yet studies in London showed that the _speed_ of travel in central
London has been quite constant since Roman times.  Except for last
weekend down Regents Street, a car travels no faster on average than a
Roman cart used to.

> place I worked went bankrupt. 

Apparently, Robert, you too worked at places that were even more
subnormal.

> I wasn't the problem, but the same defective hiring practices
> produced employees who were. 

Is it possible that you got the job because everyone ahead of you
noticed the 'defective hiring practices' and the imminent bankrupcy ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Opportunity Available

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-07-05T18:04:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cccj8l$psp$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d5ca4930.0406291129.3ab05e55@posting.google.com> <10eir4dg319ej48@corp.supernews.com> <ccc6ot$8lk$1@panix5.panix.com> <10ejf312la7h835@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <10ejf312la7h835@corp.supernews.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <10eir4dg319ej48@corp.supernews.com>,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc., if you're spending less, you can save more.

That was never questioned... what is questioned is 'how *much* more?'

>Now me, I'd probably 
>take the $100K job in Atlanta versus the $65K in Jackson, because you 
>would make out better.  But, you wouldn't feel like you were doing 
>$35K/year better.

It depends on the circumstances you choose... compare 

<http://www.homefair.com/homefair/servlet/ActionServlet?pid=200&tool=salarycalculator&previousPage=116&cid=homefair&fromState=MS&toState=GA&salary=65&fromCity=2836000&toCity=1304000&ownrent=own>

... with ...


<http://www.homefair.com/homefair/servlet/ActionServlet?pid=200&tool=salarycalculator&previousPage=116&cid=homefair&fromState=MS&toState=GA&salary=65&fromCity=2836000&toCity=1304000&ownrent=own>

... and this shows that owning a home at US$65K/yr in Jackson is the 
equivalent of owning a home at US$84K/yr in Atlanta... but *renting* in 
Jackson at US$64K/yr in Jackson is the equivalent of renting in Atlanta at 
US$73K/yr.

DD
```

<a id="t-49e6b744e6"></a>
## Thread: Method to force keeping of source — 8 this month / 16 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2004-06](2004-06.md#t-49e6b744e6)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Method to force keeping of source

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-07-01T03:14:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40E3815F.5070303@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cbv55o$5b1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cbsaiv$18sk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0RpEc.47473$OT6.19203109@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <cbuu10$31e7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cbuvch$6td$1@panix5.panix.com> <cbv327$3a0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cbv55o$5b1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I don't know that this has anything to do with this subject, but
here is an actual case....

Corporate has multiple sites with IT services. For this story
you only need to know that one was running a corporate wage payroll
on an IBM 7010.

To plan for backup, and load sharing, a decision was made to
recompile the programs on the IBM 7xxx at the to be backup
location.

Since the IBM 7xxx had a bundle of registers, and other features,
the compiler supplied by IBM took advantage of the hardware features.
I'm half way sure that LE must have come form several situations like 
this as, what happened next was:

Effort to install the backup site, and off load some from the fist site,
was delayed by many months.

Cause= the compilers were built by different teams, and to the hardware.

When this COBOL CODASYL person was asked to explain to the VP 
responsible for all IT installations what was wrong with COBOL, as
a good soldier, he said it's not our fault, it's IBM's.
To which he responded somewhat: You better care if IBM is
at fault, your pension fund had a lot of their stock.

All of a sudden, the reason for leasing the incompatible
other computer became clear, and it is possible there
was egg on a lot of faces.

Think about the PC and how it has changed the market.

Warren

Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 30-Jun-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> So what do I need to do to be as smart as a CEO?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Method to force keeping of source

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-07-01T03:35:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PDLEc.931$R36.519@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<cbsaiv$18sk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0RpEc.47473$OT6.19203109@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <cbuu10$31e7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cbuvch$6td$1@panix5.panix.com> <cbv327$3a0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cbv55o$5b1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <40E3815F.5070303@optonline.net>`

```
Warren,
   In all my years of working at IBM mainframe sites, for software vendors, and
with SHARE/GUIDE - I heard of one (exactly ONE) person/site that actually used
the (required by FIPS) "flagging" mechanism to find out which extensions they
were using in their source code.

That is one MORE site than I ever heard of using the Micro Focus or Fujitsu
facility for doing "extension flagging".  Given that sites don't (apparently)
care about writing "portable" source code - or that they don't think that the
ANSI/ISO/FIPS language definitions are the "real-world" standard language,  I
question how much benefit there would ever be in "improving" the standardization
of the language.  The lack of using this flagging pre-dates the '85 Standard,
much less the (still not fully implemented) 2002 Standard.

FYI,
  It is also interesting how many of the current COBOL compiler vendors aren't
even "on" the committee(s) any more, e.g. AcuCorp, Liant, LegacyJ, HP
(representing Dec, Tandem, and the original HP). CA,  "OpenCOBOL", etc).  When
considered with the fact that of the three vendors currently on J4 (IBM, Micro
Focus, Unisys) only Micro Focus has publicly committed to EVER producing a 2002
conforming compiler - and I am not certain that they still state this.

Maybe I am agreeing with you (I am not positive exactly what you are
"advocating") - but the Standardization process simply doesn't seem "relevant"
anymore  - or at least not very.

NOTE:
  The "death" of FIPS/NIST certification and US government "requirements" for
conforming compilers seems highly relevant to this - and this PRE-dated the
"delays" in the delivery of the 2002 Standard.
```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Warren,
>   In all my years of working at IBM mainframe sites, for software vendors, and
>with SHARE/GUIDE - I heard of one (exactly ONE) person/site that actually used
>the (required by FIPS) "flagging" mechanism to find out which extensions they
>were using in their source code.

In 42 years, I've seen zero.

>That is one MORE site than I ever heard of using the Micro Focus or Fujitsu
>facility for doing "extension flagging".  Given that sites don't (apparently)
>care about writing "portable" source code - or that they don't think that the
>ANSI/ISO/FIPS language definitions are the "real-world" standard language,  I
>question how much benefit there would ever be in "improving" the
standardization
>of the language.  The lack of using this flagging pre-dates the '85 Standard,
>much less the (still not fully implemented) 2002 Standard.

The Standard has a lot of value by providing a baseline, a guaranteed minimum.
If it's in the Standard, I KNOW it will be available and work as described
there. 

I, and I think most Cobol programmers, prefer to write Standard-compliant code.
We use extensions when there is a good reason, not because it's easier or we
don't know better. 

>FYI,
>  It is also interesting how many of the current COBOL compiler vendors aren't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>conforming compiler - and I am not certain that they still state this.

I think it's inevitable that Micro Focus and IBM will .. because they see
themselves as flag-bearers. Probably Fujitsu and Open Source too .. because they
don't carry the baggage of historical debates. To them, it's just another
technical spec.

>Maybe I am agreeing with you (I am not positive exactly what you are
>"advocating") - but the Standardization process simply doesn't seem "relevant"
>anymore  - or at least not very.

Mr. Dashwood will be delighted to read that sentiment.

>NOTE:
>  The "death" of FIPS/NIST certification and US government "requirements" for
>conforming compilers seems highly relevant to this - and this PRE-dated the
>"delays" in the delivery of the 2002 Standard.

That government dethroned itself is a Good Thing for the software world. They
weren't innovators, they were choosy and autocratic consumers. 

The Real World is much bigger then government and more meritocratic. It wants
Standards, whether or not government flexes bureaucratic muscle.
```

Also top posting
by Warren

Again, Chuck,,,, I believe I understand your position and perhaps my 
points are not clear enough to obtain interest. My view has been outside
of compiler production and maintenance.

It is probably clear that I believe that the development of COBOL has 
been cut short compared to the development of the offerings of vendors.
My suggestion that the record description be part of the file is based
upon years of reading C.L.C. and personal experience with how shops are
run, not how they should be.  I believe COBOL is a good thing and 
deserves to grow beyond the limits of a vendor biased group. O.K. you
all don't feel like you are biased. Do you feel like you have kept
pace with the needs of a common business programming language? My point
is that the effort is only current with the hardware and software
vendors that support them. All kinds of new things happen that these
vendors will never think to do something about unless a new idea
or two comes along.

I don't think for a moment that vendors would like to provide equal
data representation. There is to much money to be make by not overcoming 
the differences. This is a large step away from the current status.

Yet, the tower of babbling goes on with more new tools to do the
same thing. I know something about the development of the Burroughs
lines and well as the Univac and the IBM lines. Outstanding work has 
been done by each, and I've seen the systems they have developed uses in 
mixed
situations. When they come together along with the other high end 
systems, it's likely to satisfy a DOD or other agency requirement.
I do not see that going on with the organization driving COBOL 
development because I believe there are functions in a general sense
that should be associated with COBOL AND MADE STANDARD.

As I've said, these are my opinions. I guess I side with those who feel
there is too much delay in flushing out the capabilities normally
expected with a compiler these days. That feeling drives me to wonder
why, and my only conclusion is the objective is to revert to status quo. 
The concept and success of COBOL will fad like other tools. I guess we 
just need to have something going on for the colleges to teach that has 
a better ring to it. We need IT departments who make the same mistakes 
all over again in support of something new. Label me disappointed. Given 
the magic wand, there would be changes. As we used to complain, the only 
thing wrong with *what ever* is that " they won't let me*us run it."

You should see this new gadget I just got for my computer. Holds the
equivalent of 88 floppy disks, and looks like a pack of gum.

Warren Simmons

<a id="t-7f106de644"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Copybook Parsing — 61 msgs

### Cobol Copybook Parsing

- **From:** ed_narayanan@yahoo.com (Ed)
- **Date:** 2004-07-01T07:50:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
I am a java programmer trying to parse cobol copybooks and convert
them to xsd.
I was wondering if there were any sample copybooks and associated data
available anywhere on the net ? The problem is that I am still wading
thru cobol, so I tht those samples would help clear some doubts.
Either ways if anyone else has an idea then please answer/confirm the
following:

1. Firstly I have concluded that all fields in cobol are fixed
length(except for some DEPENDING ON stuff taht I have to read.). What
would be the size of a field which has a V(decimal point). Would the
data in the file have a "." or only the digits.

2. Similarly for edited numerics and alphanumerics would the data file
contain the edits like $,. etc. i.e are they in the data or are they
inserted.

3.  PIC S9(5)V9(2) ... Would the size of this be 7(5+2) ?

4. Is data arranged sequentially in these files, one record after
another with no delimeters?

Thanks,
Ed
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-07-01T08:52:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Ed" <ed_narayanan@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
> I am a java programmer trying to parse cobol copybooks and convert
> them to xsd.

It'd be a good idea to scrounge up a COBOL reference manual or textbook
somewhere.  I suspect it'd help a lot.

COBOL files that are the targets of the COPY verb can basically contain
anything, up to and including the entirety of a program.  The use of COPY is
not limited to data descriptions.

> I was wondering if there were any sample copybooks and associated data
> available anywhere on the net ? The problem is that I am still wading
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> data in the file have a "." or only the digits.

"V" can only appear in an *unedited* numeric PICTURE; it tells you where the
decimal point is when the data item is manipulated.  It doesn't appear in
the data.

> 2. Similarly for edited numerics and alphanumerics would the data file
> contain the edits like $,. etc. i.e are they in the data or are they
> inserted.

$ is one of a few special cases in that if there's more than one of them in
the PICTURE character-string it indicates that it can float, but yes, they
are in the data:
    01  A PIC S9(5)V99 value -12345.67.
    01  B PIC $$$,$$$9.99-.
    MOVE A TO B.
B will contain "$12,345.67-".
    MOVE 37.2 TO B.
Where the character "b" represents a blank space, B will contain
"bbbb$37.2b".

> 3.  PIC S9(5)V9(2) ... Would the size of this be 7(5+2) ?

Presuming it's USAGE DISPLAY, and there's no applicable SIGN clause on the
item that states otherwise, yes, the size of the item would be seven
characters.    Were it USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL, it's most likely the size would
be eight four-bit digits (one of which is the sign).

> 4. Is data arranged sequentially in these files, one record after
> another with no delimeters?

Fields are arranged in records in memory without delimiters (though there
may be "slack bits" in some implementations and in some circumstances
between two data items).  How records are arranged in files is
implementor-dependent and hardware-dependent (where are the files?).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

- **From:** ed_narayanan@yahoo.com (Ed)
- **Date:** 2004-07-02T05:32:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com> <cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Hey,
Chuck, Robert, Richard...Thanks for the explanantions. The thing you
all mentioned is the system dependent data file format. Now this is a
problem cause if you have just a data description copybook and its
associated data you have to be able to parse the data somehow.

I installed weblogic workshop which has a copybook importer.
http://e-docs.bea.com/wlintegration/v2_0/xmltranslator/userguid/import.htm
This sucks in copybook and spits out a bea proprietary file called
MFL(Messg. format lang.) The only information it asks for are about
Endianess(big/little) and encoding(ASCII/EBCDIC/Other(?)). So I would
assume that this is all u need along with a copybook to parse a data
file.

So now comes the part where all of you have talked about implementor
dependent/proprietary file system/file org./compiler :

>  How records are arranged in files is
> implementor-dependent and hardware-dependent (where are the files?).
>     -Chuck Stevens

>First it will depend on the file
>organization and then it may depend on the brand of the compiler used
>and also on several options.

>Some Cobols use a
>proprietary file system that adds a 2-4 byte header to each record. 

 So am a bit puzzled about this as the bea importer doesnt ask for any
more info. Wonder how they handle the different formats then ?
Regards,
Ed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

- **From:** robert.deletethis@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2004-07-02T16:39:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40e58ba0.612698847@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com> <cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com>`

```
ed_narayanan@yahoo.com (Ed) wrote:

>Hey,
>Chuck, Robert, Richard...Thanks for the explanantions. The thing you
>all mentioned is the system dependent data file format. Now this is a
>problem cause if you have just a data description copybook and its
>associated data you have to be able to parse the data somehow.

If I were writing the parser, I would have it also look at the data file .. to
determine whether records have a header and whether they are ASCII terminated.

>I installed weblogic workshop which has a copybook importer.
>http://e-docs.bea.com/wlintegration/v2_0/xmltranslator/userguid/import.htm
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>more info. Wonder how they handle the different formats then ?

My guess would be it assumes IBM mainframe rules for the grey areas. Some major
PC Cobol compilers do that so programs can be developed and tested on a PC, then
uploaded to a mainframe. 

The most commonly used grey areas on IBM are:

COMP-5  is the same as BINARY. They may be small-endian when the compiler runs
on a small-endian machine such as Intel. 

Size of PIC          Bytes allocated
1-4                      2
5-9                      4
>9                       8

COMP-3 is the same as PACKED DECIMAL. IBM mainframe rules require a sign be
allocated and size rounded up to integral bytes (because that's what IBM
handware requires). 

Try giving it an uncommon USAGE such as COMP-1. If that translates to
single-precision float, the translator is following IBM rules. If it chokes on
COMP-X, a Micro Focus feature, or PIC 1, that would be further evidence the
author didn't consider other Cobol compilers.
```

Ed wrote:
> 
> So now comes the part where all of you have talked about implementor
> dependent/proprietary file system/file org./compiler :
> 
> 

Do you not have access to someone cognizant of COBOL who can write a q&d
program to convert the files to ASCII format?  Seems to me you must have
the files as well as the copy books: it would not be difficult to read
them and write them out as fixed-length fields or as comma-seperated
fields ( with all numeric fields unpacked) .  Then Bob's your uncle!

PL

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
> Do you not have access to someone cognizant of COBOL who can write a q&d
> program to convert the files to ASCII format?  Seems to me you must have
> the files as well as the copy books: it would not be difficult to read

The way I understand it so far is that the cobol data files can
contain both text(EBCDIC/ASCII) AND binary(numerics with cOMP). So i
assume a simple ebcdic to ascii would not suffice. Usually data files
are either binary or text ! This one has a mixture of both :( So what

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-07-05T19:29:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40E9F268.CA6EDF66@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com> <cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com> <40E5917E.19CF93E6@mb.sympatico.ca> <64c2ece4.0407050851.23613e55@posting.google.com>`

```
Ed wrote:
> 
> Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> do we call such a file ?

Piece of cake.  Cobol converts all its numeric formats to ordinary
numbers with decimal point and sign as required, using a MOVE
statement.  If your files actually contain EBCDIC then the compiler in
question will support EBCDIC -> ASCII conversions.  (Even IBM mainframe
environments, n'est-ce pas?)

At least TRY finding a programmer.  There must be zillions of us.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-07-06T16:31:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0407061531.72befbec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com> <cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com> <40E5917E.19CF93E6@mb.sympatico.ca> <64c2ece4.0407050851.23613e55@posting.google.com>`

```
ed_narayanan@yahoo.com (Ed) wrote 

> Usually data files
> are either binary or text ! This one has a mixture of both :( So what
> do we call such a file ?

Usually, 'binary' files also include text.  For example if you look at
an executable file it will be mostly binary but there will usually be
text strings for messages, etc.

There may well be some files that are only numbers stored as binary,
but data files could be a mixture of text and binary or all text.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<9CD6igzuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<64c2ece4.0407010650.45e1c30d@posting.google.com> <cc1c0p$1l38$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com>`

```
It seems, my NetNews provider is again sloppy on forwarding my  
messages to comp.lang.cobol; none of my contributions to this thread  
appears in groups.google.com

That's why I resubmit them. It it is a duplicate for any of you readers, please let me know via email, including, if possible, the original message with all its headers.
------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

.    Am  02.07.04
schrieb  ed_narayanan@yahoo.com (Ed)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  64c2ece4.0407020432.5b123882@posting.google.com
  ueber  Re: Cobol Copybook Parsing

en> So now comes the part where all of you have talked about implementor
en> dependent/proprietary file system/file org./compiler :

   Take into account that those who talk COBOL here have worked most  
of their time on big iron, where there are quite important differences  
in machine architecture and where each and every hardware vendor  
produced his own operating system and his own compilers, including a  
COBOL compiler.


en>  So am a bit puzzled about this as the bea importer doesnt ask for
en> any more info. Wonder how they handle the different formats then ?

   I don't know, but this piece of software might be somewhat  
adventurous in this regard.

   But it may also be that in the PC world the COBOL compilers are not  
so diverse, and that a certain industry-standard has developed among  
the different software houses offering COBOL compilers for the  
derivatives of MS-DOS and UNIX.

   And I guess - otherwise you would have mentioned it -- that your  
work is based on this Intel/Microsoft or Intel/Linux world.

   For example, the standard (85) knows as USAGE only COMP or  
COMPUTATIONAL, DISPLAY, and INDEX.

   Implementors have implemented a number of extensions with more  
computational formats like COMP-1, COMP-2, COMP-3 etc. Now, several  
vendors have accepted IBM's definition of COMP-3 as meaning packed- 
decimal.

   So, I think it is possible, but I don't know, that all those Micro  
Focus, Fujitsu, Realia etc COBOLs for PCs treat those COMP-n USAGEs in  
an identical fashion.

   If this translator bases itself on such knowledge, OK.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Es sind nicht die Generï¿½le und Kï¿½nige, die die Geschichte machen,
sondern die breiten Massen des Volkes"                - Nelson Mandela
```
